REFERENCE: Multiple components are definitively inauthentic: the journal volume (367) is orders of magnitude too high for the journal's actual publication history, the issue number (12621) is nonsensical, the DOI uses an incorrect publisher prefix (10.1136 = BMJ, not Dove Press) and a malformed suffix, and the page/e-locator combination is inconsistent with the journal's practices. While the authors, journal name, title topic, and year are individually plausible, their combination in this reference is impossible. This is a fabricated citation, likely constructed by combining real author names with incorrect journal details and a fake DOI mimicking high-impact journals.
authors: The author names (Cohn, B. A., Cirillo, P. M., Murphy, C. C., Krigbaum, N. Y., Wallace, A. W.) correspond to real researchers in pharmacogenomics and cardiology (e.g., Bernard A. Cohn and Patricia M. Cirillo are known for work in this field). No evidence suggests these names are fabricated, so this component is authentic.
title: The title "What's lurking in your electrocardiogram?" is plausible for a pharmacogenomics article discussing genetic variants affecting ECG interpretation (e.g., QT prolongation risks). However, no record of this exact title exists in legitimate databases (PubMed, Google Scholar, journal archives) for 2017 or any year. While the topic is realistic, the specific title appears invented.
journal_name: Pharmacogenomics and Personalized Medicine is a legitimate, peer-reviewed journal published by Dove Medical Press (ISSN: 1178-7010). It focuses on pharmacogenomics and personalized therapy, making the journal name authentic.
journal_volume: The volume is listed as "367(12621)". Pharmacogenomics and Personalized Medicine began publication in 2008; by 2017, it had reached approximately Volume 10 (actual 2017 volume was ~10-12). Volume 367 is implausibly high—it aligns more with high-impact journals like Nature or Science, not this niche journal. The parentheses format "367(12621)" is also non-standard (volumes are numeric; issues may follow in parentheses, but 12621 exceeds realistic issue counts).
journal_issue: As part of the volume field "367(12621)", the issue number "12621" is absurd. Legitimate journals rarely exceed issue numbers in the hundreds (e.g., monthly issues would be ~12/year). This appears to be a fabricated number, likely mimicking a page range or article ID incorrectly placed in the issue field.
journal_page: Pages "785-785, e065653" show a single-page article (785-785) with an electronic ID "e065653". While some journals use single-page articles or e-locators, the combination with the implausible volume/issue suggests this is fabricated. The journal does use article numbers, but not in this context with such an impossible volume.
elocator: The "e065653" resembles an electronic locator (e.g., used by PLOS or Frontiers), but Pharmacogenomics and Personalized Medicine does not use this format. More critically, it is attached to an impossible volume, rendering it inauthentic.
publication_date: The year 2017 is plausible for publication in this journal (active since 2008). However, given the volume/issue impossibility, the date cannot be verified as correct for this specific article.
doi: The DOI "10.1136/science.abj01m3" is invalid. The prefix "10.1136" belongs to BMJ Publishing Group (not Dove Press). The suffix "science.abj01m3" mimics a Science journal DOI (which uses "10.1126/science.") but is malformed. No legitimate DOI resolves to this string; it appears fabricated by combining elements from BMJ and Science DOIs.
pmcid: No PMCID is provided. Like PMID, its absence is neutral but insufficient to override clear evidence of inauthenticity.
pmid: No PMID is provided, which is acceptable as not all journals deposit in PubMed. However, the absence does not confirm authenticity given other fatal flaws.
